stages.heading: Stardate 2227.00X
commentary: Welcome to a rematch of AI Solitaire Experienced. Since we have used the Talon faction these past 2 battles, we will be hand picking Terran this time. To finish learning what else there is of the known Talon-verse (at least, on the tactical side), we will also be hand picking a lone black hole terrain counter, and one of the 500 SP fleets present in the Talon 1000 book.

We field 1 Carrier, 4 Fighters, 1 Battlecruiser-H, 2 Scouts, and 1 Scout-E. Hilariously, this fleet has no anti-matter torpedos. (Probably because our Battlecruiser variant replaces a pair of them with a Wave-Motion Gun, the latter of which we intend to master.)
<hr />

With the assistance of a +10% handicap, the AI rolls a 3, fielding 1 Battleship, 3 Light Cruisers, and 1 Destroyer. They then roll a 1 for distance (uses the row closest to our deployment zone), a 1 for side (west side), and a 4 for formation (by type).

Since the initial layout is not balanced, we then have to draw their ships. We initially draw their sole Battleship, so it gets placed first. We then draw one of their Light Cruisers, so we place them all in a row. This leaves only their single Destroyer, which simply gets placed accordingly.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01A
commentary: Since this is our first time using fighters, this battle will be a little denser than the previous.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01B
commentary: Fighters move quickly, as part of their ability to swarm in numbers; but they are also very squishy.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01C
commentary: They cannot quite use their cobalt cannons. We could use our phasers, but we reckon it is probably safer to wait a bit.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01D (AI Turn)
commentary: And now for the fun part: Normally the AI would do an alpha strike of all of its weapon groups against our fleet. But, they are using their weapons against our fighters; and it is possible they might be able to use most of their cobalt cannons against our bigger ships. Thus, they decide to use only the right-most cobalt cannon, along with all the lasers, against our fighters. Probably for the best, given that black hole interference may adversely affect their accuracy.
<hr />

Since their Destroyer is quite limited, it will go first. It fires its 2 lasers against Squadron E5, having rolled 1,6. It is at least as close to the black hole than its target (3 hexes ≤ 4); so it gets no penalties, and deals 3 damage total.

Light Cruiser G6 is next. It takes a risk and uses its first laser against the intact fighter in Squadron E5. It rolls a 4, which backfires for 1 damage. It then directs the rest of its weapons against Squadron E4. Its last laser rolled a 6 (success), combined with no accuracy penalties; allowing it to destroy another 2 fighters.

Going a little out of order, Light Cruiser G3 is next. Since only Squadron E4 is in range, it uses its lasers one at a time. The first roll is a 5, which would have dealt 2 damage... except black hole interference reduces this roll (-1 modifier), so it only deals 1 damage. It then has to use its other laser, rolling a 2 and barely scraping that last hit in...
Light Cruiser G4 finally gets its segment, only the remaining targets in range are just 2 damaged fighters. With no penalty this time, 2 lasers can clean them up without fail.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01D (Player Turn)
commentary: Although not the most favorable; by waiting until now to use our fighter phasers, we will have a better chance of damaging/destroying a ship. We will focus on Light Cruiser G4 for this impulse. Our Fighters rolled 6,2,2,2,3,2. With no penalties this time, we deal 7 damage total; draining their front shield while dealing 2 non-critical hull damage.

Finally, this is also the earliest opportunity we can use our Wave-Motion Gun. Accuracy is good, even at range 4 (≥3 to hit). Against Light Cruiser G3, we rolled a 3. Normally, this would have been a hit. But black hole interference causes our roll to have a -1 modifier (9>6), so we instead miss. Oh it would have been such a satisfying kill, too!

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01E
commentary: This impulse E is not empty, because they can already use more of their cobalt cannons. Their Battleship takes advantage of where our scouts are positioned (no penalties), being able to destroy Scout D1 without fail.

Against Scout D3, a low roll will not necessarily destroy them, so we have to roll normally. A 3, not quite high enough to kill it. This forces Light Cruiser G3 to also use a cobalt cannon and clean up the latter scout.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01F
commentary: Although typically the busiest impulse in the round, its usually restricted to movement and power allocation. Most weapon groups have already been expended by this point. They have one last Cobalt Cannon that they can use, against our Scout-E. Once again, a low roll could screw them. They rolled a 6, which easily destroys it. They also use their last Laser, against Squadron E4. It rolled a 2, barely dealing 1 damage thanks to black hole interference.
<hr />

At this point, we really only have one relatively safe bet to make, and even it has a small risk of failing. We use our Battlecruiser-H's Phasers against their battleship, having rolled 2,5. Even with black hole interference, we still connected 3 damage.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.01X
commentary: Despite the losses we already sustained, we have a little more than half strength. Then again, our Battlecruiser-H makes up almost half of this figure. We shift said ship down, and nothing else.

Meanwhile, the AI already effectively has our remaining fighter squadrons in their sides/rears. They have a considerable Estimated Fleet Value advantage (6:11 in their favor). Even after having exhausted options, we still have a tie to break.

We resolve it by starting with their battleship. It rolled a 4, binding Squadron F4 to it. Since the EFV total of ships with a priority target now exceeds the total of PPTs (4>2), we end this step anyway. Roughly half their ships shift down, whether it be because the assigned target is not in front, or because no targets are in front at all.

Now that the power phase is done, the black hole carries out its unique ability in between rounds. All ships have been shifted one hex closer to it! Straight edges (from the center of one hex to the center of another) were used to determine where a ship could go. All available shifts were limited to 1 choice each this time.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.02A
commentary: Now that their lasers have recharged, they are ready to use them once more. We start with their Destroyer. It focuses on the one fighter that has already gained power from our carrier. A 2 was rolled, which was not enough. It then simply finishes this fighter off to deny us that phaser. Continuing to the adjacent Light Cruiser, it uses its first Laser against the adjacent squadron, which has a damaged fighter. It simply finishes this off.
Since it is potentially possible to damage our carrier's hull, the remaining Light Cruiser lasers are used as such. Against our front, they rolled 3,4,5 (4 dmg). Against our left flank, 1,5 (3 dmg).

That leaves only the Battleship. There is a tie even after exhausting all options, so it randomly chooses the farther squadron. It rolled a 2, which barely did 1 damage thanks to black hole interference.
<hr />
During our segment, we actively charge one of the Battlecruiser-H's phasers, and use it against their battleship. We rolled a 4, which did 1 damage thanks to interference denying us a deuce.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.02B
commentary: AI movement has gotten a bit awkward, due to the effort they make to avoid collision.

Initially, we understood the Talon 1000 rulebook to mean that the AI is entirely immune to damage this way, but this is probably incorrect. Instead, their ships move simultaneously; versus our necessity to move one ship at a time. While they do still attempt to get as close to our ships as possible, Priority 1 is avoiding collisions with their own.
With the previous two elements in mind, they can take advantage of any hex that is expected to be open. But if a ship of theirs is not slated to move, it instead acts as a wall.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.02C
commentary: An opportunity presents itself in which we can use the Phasers currently available. We start with our Fighter squadrons against their destroyer, starting at the squadron closest to the black hole. Its one phaser available rolls a 1... 1 damage. The next squadron then rolls 1,5... one of them missed thanks to the interference, but the other dealt 2 damage anyway.
Our bigger ships then use their phasers against their battleship. The carrier rolled 4,5 (4 dmg), while the Battlecruiser-H rolled a 3 (1 dmg).

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.02D
commentary: One of these days, we will get back in their destroyer's rear...

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.02F
commentary: Assuming this improved understanding of the collision system is correct, the AI may be more vulnerable than we give it credit.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.02X
commentary: True, we are still at a disadvantage overall. Nonetheless, we do no changes to our power curves.
As for the AI; each of our ships happen to be in front of at least one of their ships, so we have no PPTs this time. They have shifted their power curves accordingly.

And now for the fun part. The black hole now takes its segment. When a straight edge aligns with a spine to where it causes 2 equal choices, the ship owner gets to choose which hex to move to when the black hole does its magic.

Two AI ships have collided in the very same hex thanks to the involuntary movement caused by the terrain. We infer this effect is applied simultaneously for the AI... which means their destroyer takes it on the right side (3 shields → 0), while their battleship (right side already exposed) takes 3 non-critical hull damage.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03A (AI Turn)
commentary: Now that their lasers have recharged once more, they use them. Starting with their Battleship, they use their sole laser to finish off a fighter that is already damaged.
Against our carrier, their lasers rolled 1,2 against our right flank (2 dmg), and 2,3 against our rear (also 2 dmg, despite black hole interference).
Against our Battlecruiser-H, they rolled 1,1,4,4 (4 dmg).

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03A (Player Turn)
commentary: Would it not be a shame if we were able to use our Wave-Motion Gun... against their battleship? That is true for this impulse!

Granted, black hole interference causes it to be a 50-50, but this is the best impulse to attempt this shot. We rolled a 6... which displaces their ship! Because we are firing along a spine, we can choose between one of two hexes... which means we can push their battleship into the black hole!! This is fortunate, as we would have otherwise dealt 1 non-critical hull damage after emptying their rear shields.

Moving onto our fighters, we use our remaining Phasers; starting against Light Cruiser C7. Our farther squadron rolled a 6 (2 dmg). Our closer squadron then rolled a 1 (1 dmg), which necessitates they roll for critical. 5+6 would normally cause Power Loss, but the AI would not be affected by this penalty. Instead, they take an extra damage. One last phaser then can be used to finish this ship off, and without fail. Explosion damage is then applied.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03B
commentary: Eliminating their largest threat may have made a huge difference in our performance this battle. Not to mention we denied them 3 cobalt cannons in total, especially 1 round before they would otherwise be ready for use.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03C
commentary: The current reprieve will end soon...

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03D
commentary: We have another opportunity, but it is risky. Phasers are not reliable at range 3 (small chance to do 1 damage), but they may still make just the difference we need. Starting from right-to-left, we rolled a 6... which did 1 damage anyway and necessitates a critical roll. 1+2 (Shields Down). We then roll another 4... a miss.
We also squeeze impulse E on this stage, so that their ship can do a repair check on Shields Down. A 3 was rolled (failure).

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03F
commentary: We take advantage of their destroyer's vulnerable state. We charge one of our Carrier's phaser banks, and use it against their ship. We rolled a 5, dealing 2 damage and finishing their ship off. Since the ship with Shields Down is destroyed, no more repair checks are necessary.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.03X
commentary: One very fortunate wave-motion gun hit, combined with 2 other ships being destroyed the regular way, allowed us to take a significant lead. Furthermore, depending on how well we do the rest of this battle, we may still be able to secure a tactical victory. The best they can achieve now is just a marginal victory.

While we do no changes to our power curves, the AI is in a bit of a pickle. We got 2 ships beyond their front arcs, so they assign them as PPTs. The EFV totals have also shifted in our favor (6:4). The Light Cruiser closest to our battlecruiser rolled a 3... a failure under these new modifiers. No priority targets are assigned. Neither of their ships have a reason to be at <u>0-3-0</u>, so they shift back up to <u>0-4-1</u>.
Finally, the black hole does its pull. Since most of the remaining ships are relatively directly west of the black hole, they get shifted accordingly.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.04A
commentary: Now that the remaining AI weapon groups have all recharged, they are ready to use them. They use their first Laser against one of our fighters. A 4... good for 1 damage despite black hole interference. It was not enough, so they have to roll their second laser. A 2, barely dealing another 1 damage. Light Cruiser G6 then uses its sole Cobalt Cannon against our battlecruiser, rolling a 5... and barely dealing 5 damage thanks to the terrain.

Their other Light Cruiser then uses its weapons against our very same ship, only this time the black hole will not decrease its accuracy. Cobalt Cannon goes first, rolling a 6 for another 5 damage. We must roll on the critical table. 3+4 = 7. A lucky break. It then uses one of its Lasers to finish our ship off. Their adjacent ship takes 3 explosion damage in return.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.04B
commentary: Our largest ship in the fleet is also gone. This basically puts the advantage back in their hands. Our remaining Fighters use their Phasers against the light cruiser occupying the same hex as our carrier. 5,6 were rolled, dealing 4 damage to their rear... including 1 non-critical hull damage.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.04C
commentary: The AI is now able to use its last Laser, which it uses against our damaged fighter.

Now that an opportunity has presented itself, we can use our Carrier's Phasers against the light cruiser we just damaged. From rear to rear, we rolled a 6. Even with black hole interference, that still deals 2 damage, and necessitates they roll for critical. 3+4 (None). We then roll another 5, dealing 2 more damage and finishing them off too!

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.04D
commentary: With the semi-final AI ship destroyed, this means we are now roughly even strength.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.04F
commentary: Soon it will be time to regroup... and also be dragged.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.04X
commentary: Overall, this is a close battle. Only a marginal victory is possible now for either team.
We do no changes to our power curves, still. Meanwhile, our last fighter is a PPT. A 1 was rolled (failure), so it does not get designated. Thus, their Light Cruiser stays at <u>0-4-1</u>.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.05A
commentary: Their Lasers recharge once more. They use them against our carrier, having rolled 3,6 (3 dmg). We sustained 1 non-critical hull damage.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.05B
commentary: Not much to do, now that there are just 3 ships on the board.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.05C
commentary: We could have used the single Fighter phaser earlier in the round, but are saving it for their right-side... if we can help it.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.05D
commentary: But, it is not easy when their light cruiser is only just a little slower than fighter.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.05F
commentary: The opportunity has presented itself. We can now make them pay for their insolence with our remaining Phasers. Our Fighter rolls a 2, dealing 2 damage because it gets no accuracy penalty.

We take advantage of the rear weapon arcs, and use them against their light cruiser also, only there is a -1 accuracy penalty. Still, we rolled 4,5, dealing 3 more damage and necessitating a critical roll. 2+3 (Random Weapon Group Destroyed). Their unlucky weapon group is none other than their cobalt cannon!

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.05X
commentary: We have gained considerable momentum with this round. By being smart with our remaining weapons, we have knocked their light cruiser to near destruction (1 hull point left).
We still keep our power curves stable. On the PPT step, they rolled a 3... which still fails even with even EFV. Due to hull damage, they are limited to 0-3-0 from here on out.
The black hole's pull continues, but it literally just shifts our ships one hex right.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06A
commentary: During their segment, they use their Lasers against our last fighter. They rolled a 2... which was not enough, so they must burn their other laser to clean it up.
During our segment, we recharge one of our Carrier's Phaser banks, and use it against their last ship. We rolled a 1... which missed thanks to black hole interference. So close!

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06B
commentary: Despite our accuracy being extremely low here, if we successfully overcome the odds, we win this battle! We rolled a 4... which was not nearly enough.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06C
commentary: This is not good... our Carrier is all alone, and we are not even remotely facing our last target. This is one of the few ships that literally has rear-ward firing weapons.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06D
commentary: We can accelerate our turn, at least...

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06F
commentary: So that it enters our side once more.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06X
commentary: Overall, this has left us with a important decision. To keep on trying, or to just cut the losses and retreat; taking advantage of the fact that our carrier has just finished spooling.

The good news is we have no fighters left to worry about docking, so we can safely choose the latter and not incur any additional losses. As such, we do just that, and get our carrier out of the conflict zone.

stages.heading: Stardate 2227.06X (Game Over)
commentary: Thus, no sense in dragging a battle unnecessarily. Still, this was a very close battle; perfectly winnable if we had a few more dice aligned just right. They had just 1 hull point left, but their vulnerable quadrant is no longer exposed.

Still, it is useful to be able to learn nearly the rest of the rules. From a tactical standpoint, wave-motion guns, carriers, and black holes were among the last few elements that we did not already learn. We hand-picked terrain and one of the fleets that covered these elements as a result.

Transports still have not been covered. And special battles can be very tricky to arrange, so we do not see ourselves pursuing this aspect anytime soon. While we did practice with a starbase, it ended badly enough that we conceded the battle, and never published it.
